docs.title: An open-source Vision AI stack for real-world applications
docs.text:
swatahVision is an open-source Vision AI stack that brings together models, runtimes, post-processing, tracking, and visualization into a clean, reusable Python package.

It’s built to make Vision AI practical: load a model, run inference, get structured outputs, visualize results, and ship pipelines faster — without reinventing glue-code every time.

Part of the VisionAI4Bhārat initiative.

docs.title: What is swatahVision?
docs.text:
swatahVision provides a unified interface for:

  • Inference across multiple runtimes (e.g., ONNX Runtime, OpenVINO)
  • Vision tasks
  • Object Detection
  • Image Classification
  • (Extensible for OCR / segmentation / multimodal pipelines)
  • Post-processing adapters
  • YOLO-style decoders + NMS
  • SSD-style decoders
  • RetinaNet-style decoders
  • Tracking
  • Integrated ByteTrack support to assign stable tracker_id across frames
  • Visualization
  • Built-in drawing utilities for boxes, labels, and overlays
  • Video processing utilities
  • Frame generators and pipeline helpers

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• ✅ Unified model wrapper: same API for different backends/runtimes
  • ✅ Structured outputs:
  • Detections: boxes, confidence, class_id, tracker_id (and more)
  • Classification: class_id, confidence, top-k
  • ✅ Production-friendly utilities: FPS monitor, video readers/writers, batching-ready patterns
  • ✅ Lightweight & composable: designed as a stack, not a monolith
  • ✅ Real world examples and analytics
  • ✅ **Built-in annotation utilities
  • ✅ **Minimal and clean API

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/VisionAI4Bharat/swatahvision.git
cd swatahvision
pip install -e .

docs.title: Load a model and run inference
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv

model = sv.Model(
    model="path/to/model.onnx",
    engine=sv.Engine.ONNX,
    hardware=sv.Hardware.CPU
)

outputs = model(image, input_size=(640, 640))

docs.title: Convert raw outputs to Detections
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv

detections = sv.Detections.from_yolo(
    outputs,
    conf_threshold=0.3,
    nms_threshold=0.5,
    class_agnostic=False
)

print(len(detections))
print(detections.xyxy[:3])

docs.title: Filter / slice detections
docs.text:
high_conf = detections[detections.confidence > 0.6]
persons = detections[detections.class_id == 0]

docs.title: Draw boxes
docs.text:
import cv2
import swatahvision as sv

frame = cv2.imread("image.jpg")

annotated = sv.UI.draw_bboxes(
    image=frame.copy(),
    detections=detections,
    conf=0.3
)

cv2.imwrite("out.jpg", annotated)

docs.title: Box Annotator – Drawing Bounding Boxes on Images
docs.text:
This code creates a Box Annotator, which is used to draw bounding boxes on an image based on object detection results.

In simple terms, it helps visualize where objects were detected in an image.

For example, if an AI model detects objects such as:

  • cars
  • people
  • bicycles

the Box Annotator will draw rectangles around those objects.

docs.title: What is a Bounding Box?
docs.text:
A bounding box is a rectangle drawn around an object detected by an AI model.

Example:

+------------------+
|                  |
|       CAR        |
|                  |
+------------------+

The rectangle shows where the object is located in the image.

docs.title: Purpose of This Code
docs.text:
The main goal of this code is to:

  1. Take an image
  2. Take object detection results
  3. Draw rectangles around detected objects
  4. Return the annotated image

This helps visualize AI predictions clearly.

docs.title: Libraries Used
docs.text:
This code uses the following libraries:

  • NumPy → handles numerical data
  • OpenCV (cv2) → draws rectangles on images
  • SwatahVision modules → manage detections and annotation tools

docs.title: Main Class
docs.text:
The main class created in this file is:

BoxAnnotator

This class is responsible for drawing bounding boxes on images.

docs.title: Configurable Settings
docs.text: When creating a BoxAnnotator, you can configure a few options.

docs.title: 1. Color
docs.text:
Defines the color used to draw the bounding boxes.

Example:

color = ColorPalette.DEFAULT

Different objects can have different colors.

docs.title: 2. Thickness
docs.text:
Controls how thick the rectangle lines are.

Example:

thickness = 2

A higher value means thicker box borders.

docs.title: 3. Color Lookup Strategy
docs.text:
This decides how colors are assigned to objects.

Available strategies include:

  • INDEX → color based on detection index
  • CLASS → color based on object class
  • TRACK → color based on object tracking ID

docs.title: Main Function: annotate()
docs.text:
The most important function in this class is:

annotate()

This function draws the bounding boxes.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text: The function takes three inputs:

docs.title: 1. Scene
docs.text:
scene

The image where boxes will be drawn.

The image must be a NumPy array.

docs.title: 2. Detections
docs.text:
detections

This contains information about detected objects.

Each detection includes the coordinates of the bounding box.

Example format:

x1, y1, x2, y2

These represent the corners of the rectangle.

docs.title: 3. Custom Color Lookup (Optional)
docs.text:
custom_color_lookup

This allows users to manually control how colors are assigned.

If not provided, the default color strategy is used.

docs.title: How the Code Works
docs.text: The function follows these steps:

docs.title: Step 1 – Loop Through Detections
docs.text:
The code processes each detected object one by one.

for detection_idx in range(len(detections))

docs.title: Step 2 – Get Bounding Box Coordinates
docs.text:
For each object, the code extracts the rectangle coordinates.

x1, y1, x2, y2

These represent the corners of the box.

docs.title: Step 3 – Choose a Color
docs.text:
The color for the bounding box is selected using the color lookup strategy.

This ensures consistent colors across detections.

docs.title: Step 4 – Draw the Rectangle
docs.text:
The rectangle is drawn using OpenCV:

cv2.rectangle()

The rectangle is placed on the image using the coordinates from the detection.

docs.title: Step 5 – Return the Annotated Image
docs.text:
After all boxes are drawn, the updated image is returned.

return scene

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
Example of how this annotator might be used:

import swatahvision as sv

image = ...
detections = sv.Detections(...)

box_annotator = sv.BoxAnnotator()

annotated_frame = box_annotator.annotate(
    scene=image.copy(),
    detections=detections
)

This will return an image with bounding boxes drawn around detected objects.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
The complete process looks like this:

Input Image
      ↓
Object Detection Model
      ↓
Detections (coordinates of objects)
      ↓
BoxAnnotator draws rectangles
      ↓
Annotated Image Output

docs.title: Why This Is Useful
docs.text:
Bounding box annotation is important because it helps:

  • visualize AI model predictions
  • debug detection models
  • present results clearly
  • understand where objects are located

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This code creates a tool that:

  • receives detection results
  • draws bounding boxes on images
  • allows customizable colors and thickness
  • returns an annotated image

It is commonly used in computer vision applications such as object detection systems.

docs.title: Color Annotator – Highlighting Detected Objects with Color Masks
docs.text:
This code creates a Color Annotator, which highlights detected objects in an image using colored overlays.

Instead of just drawing a rectangle outline around an object, this annotator fills the bounding box with a semi-transparent color.

This helps make detected objects more visible and easier to understand.

docs.title: What This Annotator Does
docs.text:
The Color Annotator:

  1. Takes an image
  2. Takes object detection results
  3. Draws colored rectangles over detected objects
  4. Blends those rectangles with the original image

The result is an image where detected objects appear highlighted with colored masks.

docs.title: Example Idea
docs.text:
Original image:

Person walking near a car

After annotation:

Person highlighted with a color mask
Car highlighted with another color mask

The colored regions help visually identify detected objects.

docs.title: Libraries Used
docs.text:
This code uses the following libraries:

  • NumPy → handles numerical data
  • OpenCV (cv2) → draws rectangles and blends images
  • SwatahVision modules → handle detections and annotation utilities

docs.title: Main Class
docs.text:
The main class created in this file is:

ColorAnnotator

This class is responsible for drawing colored masks over detected objects.

docs.title: Configurable Settings
docs.text: When creating a ColorAnnotator, several settings can be adjusted.

docs.title: 1. Color
docs.text:
color = ColorPalette.DEFAULT

Defines the colors used for object masks.

The annotator can use:

  • a single color
  • a palette of colors for different objects

docs.title: 2. Opacity
docs.text:
opacity = 0.5

Controls how transparent the colored mask is.

Range:

0 → invisible
1 → fully solid color

Example:

0.5 → semi-transparent mask

docs.title: 3. Color Lookup Strategy
docs.text:
color_lookup = ColorLookup.CLASS

This determines how colors are assigned to objects.

Possible options:

  • INDEX → color based on detection order
  • CLASS → color based on object type
  • TRACK → color based on tracking ID

docs.title: Main Function: annotate()
docs.text:
The key function in this class is:

annotate()

This function draws colored masks on detected objects.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text: The function takes three inputs.

docs.title: 1. Scene
docs.text:
scene

The image where annotations will be drawn.

The image must be a NumPy array.

docs.title: 2. Detections
docs.text:
detections

This contains information about detected objects.

Each detection includes the coordinates of the bounding box.

Example format:

x1, y1, x2, y2

These coordinates define the rectangle around an object.

docs.title: 3. Custom Color Lookup (Optional)
docs.text:
custom_color_lookup

This allows the user to manually control how colors are assigned.

If not provided, the default color strategy is used.

docs.title: How the Code Works
docs.text: The annotation process happens in several steps.

docs.title: Step 1 – Copy the Original Image
docs.text:
A copy of the image is created.

scene_with_boxes = scene.copy()

This allows drawing masks without modifying the original image immediately.

docs.title: Step 2 – Loop Through Each Detection
docs.text:
The code processes every detected object.

for detection_idx in range(len(detections))

docs.title: Step 3 – Get Bounding Box Coordinates
docs.text:
For each detection, the code extracts the rectangle coordinates:

x1, y1, x2, y2

These represent the corners of the object box.

docs.title: Step 4 – Choose the Color
docs.text:
The code selects a color based on the chosen color strategy.

Different objects may have different colors.

docs.title: Step 5 – Draw the Colored Mask
docs.text:
A filled rectangle is drawn using OpenCV:

cv2.rectangle()

The rectangle covers the detected object.

docs.title: Step 6 – Blend with the Original Image
docs.text:
The colored masks are blended with the original image using:

cv2.addWeighted()

This creates a semi-transparent overlay.

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
Example of how this annotator can be used:

import swatahvision as sv

image = ...
detections = sv.Detections(...)

color_annotator = sv.ColorAnnotator()

annotated_frame = color_annotator.annotate(
    scene=image.copy(),
    detections=detections
)

The output will be an image where detected objects are highlighted with colored masks.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
The overall process looks like this:

Input Image
      ↓
Object Detection Model
      ↓
Detections (object coordinates)
      ↓
ColorAnnotator adds colored masks
      ↓
Annotated Image Output

docs.title: Why This Is Useful
docs.text:
Color masks help make detection results easier to understand visually.

They are useful for:

  • object detection visualization
  • video analytics
  • surveillance systems
  • autonomous driving research
  • AI demonstrations

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This code creates a tool that:

  • receives detection results
  • draws colored masks over detected objects
  • blends the masks with the original image
  • produces a clear visual representation of detected objects

It is commonly used in computer vision systems to highlight detected objects clearly.

docs.title: HeatMap Annotator – Visualizing Activity with Heatmaps
docs.text:
This code creates a HeatMap Annotator, which draws a heatmap overlay on an image based on object detections.

A heatmap shows where objects appear most often in a scene. The more frequently an object appears in a location, the hotter (more intense) that area becomes.

This helps visualize activity patterns over time.

docs.title: What is a Heatmap?
docs.text:
A heatmap is a visual representation of activity or intensity using colors.

Typical heatmap colors:

  • 🔴 Red → High activity
  • 🟡 Yellow → Medium activity
  • 🔵 Blue → Low activity

Example:

Low activity        High activity
     🔵 → 🟡 → 🔴

If many detections happen in the same area, that area becomes redder and brighter.

docs.title: Purpose of This Code
docs.text:
This code is used to:

  1. Track where objects appear in a scene
  2. Accumulate those locations over time
  3. Create a heatmap showing the most active areas
  4. Overlay that heatmap on the original image

This is useful for analyzing movement patterns.

docs.title: Libraries Used
docs.text:
The code uses the following libraries:

  • NumPy → handles numerical data
  • OpenCV (cv2) → image processing and drawing
  • SwatahVision modules → detections and annotation utilities

docs.title: Main Class
docs.text:
The main class created in this file is:

HeatMapAnnotator

This class is responsible for creating and updating a heatmap on images.

docs.title: Configurable Settings
docs.text: When creating a HeatMapAnnotator, several parameters can be adjusted.

docs.title: 1. Position
docs.text:
position = Position.BOTTOM_CENTER

This determines where the heat point is placed on each detected object.

Examples:

  • center of object
  • bottom center
  • top center

docs.title: 2. Opacity
docs.text:
opacity = 0.2

Controls how transparent the heatmap overlay is.

Range:

0 → invisible
1 → fully visible

docs.title: 3. Radius
docs.text:
radius = 40

This defines the size of the heat circle drawn for each detection.

Larger radius = larger heat spots.

docs.title: 4. Kernel Size
docs.text:
kernel_size = 25

This controls how much the heatmap is blurred.

Blurring helps create a smooth heatmap instead of sharp circles.

docs.title: 5. Heatmap Colors
docs.text:
Two values control the heatmap color range:

top_hue = 0
low_hue = 125

These represent color values in HSV color format.

Typical mapping:

Red   → High activity
Blue  → Low activity

docs.title: Main Function: annotate()
docs.text:
The main function used to draw the heatmap is:

annotate()

This function takes:

docs.title: 1. Scene
docs.text:
scene

The image where the heatmap will be drawn.

It must be an image in NumPy format.

docs.title: 2. Detections
docs.text:
detections

This contains the detected objects in the image.

Each detection provides the location of an object.

docs.title: How the Heatmap Works
docs.text: The heatmap is built step-by-step.

docs.title: Step 1 – Create Heat Mask
docs.text:
The code creates a heat mask, which stores activity levels for each pixel.

Initially it contains zeros.

self.heat_mask = np.zeros(...)

docs.title: Step 2 – Mark Detection Points
docs.text:
For each detected object:

  • a circle is drawn
  • the circle represents activity

Example:

cv2.circle()

Each detection adds heat to that location.

docs.title: Step 3 – Accumulate Heat Over Time
docs.text:
The heatmap is not reset each frame.

Instead, it keeps adding new detections:

self.heat_mask = mask + self.heat_mask

This allows the heatmap to show long-term activity patterns.

docs.title: Step 4 – Convert Heat to Colors
docs.text:
The heat values are converted into colors using the HSV color system.

This produces colors like:

Blue → Low activity
Yellow → Medium activity
Red → High activity

docs.title: Step 5 – Smooth the Heatmap
docs.text:
The heatmap is blurred using:

cv2.blur()

This creates smooth gradients between hot areas.

docs.title: Step 6 – Overlay Heatmap on Image
docs.text:
Finally, the heatmap is blended with the original image using:

cv2.addWeighted()

This creates a semi-transparent overlay.

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
Example usage in a video processing pipeline:

import swatahvision as sv

heat_map_annotator = sv.HeatMapAnnotator()

detections = ...

annotated_frame = heat_map_annotator.annotate(
    scene=image.copy(),
    detections=detections
)

This will produce an image with a heatmap overlay showing activity locations.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
The full workflow looks like this:

Input Frame
      ↓
Object Detection Model
      ↓
Detections (object positions)
      ↓
HeatMapAnnotator accumulates heat
      ↓
Heatmap overlay on image

docs.title: Why Heatmaps Are Useful
docs.text:
Heatmaps help analyze behavior and movement patterns.

Common applications include:

  • retail store analytics
  • crowd movement analysis
  • sports tracking
  • traffic monitoring
  • security surveillance

They show where activity happens most frequently.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This code creates a heatmap visualization tool that:

  • tracks object locations
  • accumulates activity over time
  • converts activity into color intensity
  • overlays the heatmap on the original image

It is commonly used in computer vision analytics systems.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The LabelAnnotator is used to display text labels on detected objects in an image or video frame. It places a text label with a background box near each detection bounding box.

Labels typically contain information such as:

  • Object class name
  • Confidence score
  • Tracker ID
  • Custom text

This annotator is commonly used in object detection and tracking visualization pipelines.

docs.title: Class: LabelAnnotator
docs.text:
The LabelAnnotator class extends _BaseLabelAnnotator and provides functionality to draw labels on detected objects.

class LabelAnnotator(_BaseLabelAnnotator)

It supports customizable:

  • Text appearance
  • Background color
  • Label positioning
  • Smart overlap avoidance
  • Multi-line labels

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
LabelAnnotator(
    color=ColorPalette.DEFAULT,
    color_lookup=ColorLookup.CLASS,
    text_color=Color.WHITE,
    text_scale=0.5,
    text_thickness=1,
    text_padding=10,
    text_position=Position.TOP_LEFT,
    text_offset=(0,0),
    border_radius=0,
    smart_position=False,
    max_line_length=None
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description color Color \| ColorPalette Background color for label box color_lookup ColorLookup Strategy for mapping colors text_color Color \| ColorPalette Color used for label text text_scale float Font size scale text_thickness int Thickness of text characters text_padding int Padding around text inside label box text_position Position Position of label relative to bounding box text_offset (int, int) Offset applied to label position border_radius int Radius for rounded label box corners smart_position bool Automatically reposition labels to avoid overlap max_line_length int \| None Maximum characters per line before wrapping

docs.title: Color Lookup Strategies
docs.text:
Label colors can be determined using different strategies.

Option Description INDEX Color determined by detection index CLASS Color determined by object class TRACK Color determined by tracker ID

docs.title: Method: annotate
docs.text:
annotate(scene, detections, labels=None, custom_color_lookup=None)

Adds labels to detected objects in the provided image.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description scene ImageType Input image (numpy.ndarray or PIL.Image) detections Detections Detection results containing bounding boxes labels list[str] Custom label text for each detection custom_color_lookup np.ndarray Optional custom color mapping

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Annotated image containing labels.

numpy.ndarray or PIL.Image.Image

docs.title: How Label Annotation Works
docs.text:
1. The detection bounding boxes are obtained.
  2. Anchor coordinates are calculated based on text_position.
  3. Label text size is computed using OpenCV.
  4. A background box is created for the label.
  5. The label text is drawn on the image.

docs.title: Smart Positioning
docs.text:
When smart_position=True, the annotator attempts to prevent label overlap by spreading label boxes.

It uses internal utilities such as:

spread_out_boxes
snap_boxes

This ensures labels remain readable even when many detections appear close together.

docs.title: Multi-Line Labels
docs.text:
Long labels can be automatically wrapped.

Example:

Person
Confidence: 0.92

This is controlled using:

max_line_length

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv

image = ...
detections = sv.Detections(...)

labels = [
    f"{class_name} {confidence:.2f}"
    for class_name, confidence
    in zip(detections['class_name'], detections.confidence)
]

label_annotator = sv.LabelAnnotator(
    text_position=sv.Position.CENTER
)

annotated_frame = label_annotator.annotate(
    scene=image.copy(),
    detections=detections,
    labels=labels
)

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
The annotator draws a text label with a colored background.

Example visualization:

┌─────────────┐
│ Person 0.92 │
└─────────────┘

Positioned near the detection bounding box.

docs.title: Rounded Label Boxes
docs.text:
If border_radius > 0, the background label box will have rounded edges.

Example:

LabelAnnotator(border_radius=10)

docs.title: Invalid Label Count
docs.text:
If the number of labels does not match the number of detections:

ValueError

This prevents mismatched annotations.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
The LabelAnnotator relies on:

  • OpenCV (cv2)
  • NumPy
  • SwatahVision geometry utilities
  • SwatahVision detection modules

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The LabelAnnotator provides a flexible way to add text labels to detection results.

Key features:

  • Customizable label appearance
  • Multi-line text support
  • Smart label positioning
  • Color mapping strategies
  • Rounded label backgrounds

It is commonly used together with:

  • BoxAnnotator
  • TraceAnnotator
  • HeatMapAnnotator

to build complete visualization pipelines for computer vision models.

docs.title: 👨‍💻 Author
docs.text: • Atharva Kotkar

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The TraceAnnotator is used to visualize the movement path of tracked objects across video frames. It draws trace lines showing where an object has traveled based on its tracker ID.

This annotator is useful for:

  • Object tracking visualization
  • Motion analysis
  • Sports analytics
  • Surveillance systems
  • Traffic monitoring

The trace is generated using the historical positions of tracked detections.

docs.title: Class: TraceAnnotator
docs.text:
The TraceAnnotator class extends BaseAnnotator and is responsible for drawing trace paths for tracked objects.

class TraceAnnotator(BaseAnnotator)

It uses tracker IDs from the Detections object to associate positions across frames.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
This annotator requires object tracking.

The Detections object must contain:

detections.tracker_id

If tracker_id is missing, the annotator will raise an error.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
TraceAnnotator(
    color=ColorPalette.DEFAULT,
    position=Position.CENTER,
    trace_length=30,
    thickness=2,
    smooth=False,
    color_lookup=ColorLookup.CLASS
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description color Color \| ColorPalette Color used for the trace line position Position Anchor position used for trace calculation trace_length int Maximum number of historical points stored for each object thickness int Thickness of the trace line smooth bool Enables spline smoothing for the trace color_lookup ColorLookup Strategy for assigning colors

docs.title: Color Lookup Options
docs.text: Option Description INDEX Color determined by detection index CLASS Color determined by object class TRACK Color determined by tracker ID

docs.title: Method: annotate
docs.text:
annotate(scene, detections, custom_color_lookup=None)

Draws the trace path on the given frame.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description scene ImageType Input image (numpy.ndarray or PIL.Image) detections Detections Detection results containing bounding boxes and tracker IDs custom_color_lookup np.ndarray Optional custom color mapping

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Annotated image with trace lines.

numpy.ndarray or PIL.Image.Image

docs.title: How Trace Works
docs.text:
1. Object detections are received from the model.
  2. Each detection is associated with a tracker ID.
  3. Historical positions for each tracker ID are stored.
  4. Lines are drawn between stored positions to create a trace path.

docs.title: Trace Smoothing
docs.text:
If smooth=True, the trace path is smoothed using B-spline interpolation from scipy.

splprep
splev

This produces smoother curves instead of straight line segments.

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv
from ultralytics import YOLO

model = YOLO("yolov8x.pt")

trace_annotator = sv.TraceAnnotator()

tracker = sv.ByteTrack()

video_info = sv.VideoInfo.from_video_path(video_path="input.mp4")

frames_generator = sv.get_video_frames_generator(source_path="input.mp4")

with sv.VideoSink(target_path="output.mp4", video_info=video_info) as sink:

    for frame in frames_generator:

        result = model(frame)[0]

        detections = sv.Detections.from_ultralytics(result)

        detections = tracker.update_with_detections(detections)

        annotated_frame = trace_annotator.annotate(
            scene=frame.copy(),
            detections=detections
        )

        sink.write_frame(frame=annotated_frame)

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
The annotator will draw a line showing the movement path of each tracked object across frames.

Example visualization:

Object Path
   ●────●────●────●

Each object keeps its own trace based on its tracker ID.

docs.title: Missing Tracker ID
docs.text:
If the Detections object does not contain tracker IDs:

ValueError: The tracker_id field is missing in the provided detections

Tracking must be enabled before using this annotator.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
The TraceAnnotator relies on the following modules:

  • OpenCV (cv2)
  • NumPy
  • SciPy
  • SwatahVision detection and drawing modules

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The TraceAnnotator provides an easy way to visualize object movement over time.

Key features:

  • Draws historical object paths
  • Works with tracking pipelines
  • Supports color customization
  • Optional smooth curves
  • Compatible with video processing pipelines

This annotator is particularly useful for applications involving object tracking and motion visualization.

docs.title: 👨‍💻 Author
docs.text: • Atharva Kotkar

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Classification module provides a unified structure for handling classification outputs from deep learning models such as MobileNet and ResNet.

It converts raw model outputs (logits or probabilities) into a standardized format containing:

  • class_id → predicted class index
  • confidence → probability score for the prediction

This allows developers to easily process predictions regardless of which classification model is used.

docs.title: Class: Classification
docs.text:
The Classification class stores classification results and provides helper methods to convert outputs from different models into a consistent format.

from dataclasses import dataclass
import numpy as np

docs.title: Attributes
docs.text:
Attribute Type Description class_id np.ndarray Predicted class indices confidence np.ndarray \| None Confidence scores for predictions

Example structure:

class_id = [3]
confidence = [0.92]

docs.title: Number of Predictions
docs.text:
The __len__() method returns the number of classifications.

def __len__(self) -> int:

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
result = Classification(
    class_id=np.array([1, 2, 3]),
    confidence=np.array([0.9, 0.7, 0.6])
)

print(len(result))

Output

3

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
Classification.from_mobilenet(mobilenet_results, top_k=None)

This method converts MobileNet model outputs into a Classification object.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description mobilenet_results tuple Model output and metadata top_k int | None Number of top predictions to return

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
The method automatically:

  1. Extracts model outputs
  2. Converts tensors to NumPy
  3. Applies softmax if logits are provided
  4. Returns predictions according to top_k

docs.title: 1️⃣ All Classes (Default)
docs.text:
Classification.from_mobilenet(results)

Returns probabilities for all classes.

Example:

class_id = [[0,1,2,3,4]]
confidence = [[0.1,0.3,0.4,0.1,0.1]]

docs.title: 2️⃣ Top-1 Prediction
docs.text:
Classification.from_mobilenet(results, top_k=1)

Returns only the highest probability class.

Example:

class_id = [2]
confidence = [0.91]

docs.title: 3️⃣ Top-K Predictions
docs.text:
Classification.from_mobilenet(results, top_k=3)

Returns the top 3 predictions.

Example:

class_id = [[2,1,4]]
confidence = [[0.91,0.05,0.03]]

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
Classification.from_resnet(resnet_results, top_k=None)

This method converts ResNet model outputs into a Classification object.

The internal processing steps are identical to the MobileNet method:

  1. Extract raw outputs
  2. Convert tensors to NumPy
  3. Normalize outputs into probabilities
  4. Select predictions based on top_k

docs.title: Example 1 — MobileNet
docs.text:
mobilenet_output = model(image)

result = Classification.from_mobilenet(mobilenet_output, top_k=1)

print(result.class_id)
print(result.confidence)

Output example

class_id = [5]
confidence = [0.94]

docs.title: Example 2 — ResNet
docs.text:
resnet_output = model(image)

result = Classification.from_resnet(resnet_output, top_k=3)

print(result.class_id)
print(result.confidence)

Output example

class_id = [[3,7,1]]
confidence = [[0.88,0.07,0.02]]

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
The module always returns predictions in the following format:

Classification(
    class_id = numpy array,
    confidence = numpy array
)

Example:

Classification(
    class_id=[2],
    confidence=[0.91]
)

docs.title: Supported Model Types
docs.text:
Currently supported models:

  • MobileNet
  • ResNet

Additional classification models can be added by implementing similar conversion methods.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: The module raises errors in the following cases:

docs.title: Invalid Output Shape
docs.text:
ValueError: Invalid output shape

This occurs if the model output is not compatible with the expected format (B, C).

docs.title: Invalid top_k
docs.text:
ValueError: top_k cannot be greater than number of classes

This prevents requesting more predictions than available classes.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The Classification module provides:

  • Standardized output format
  • Support for MobileNet and ResNet
  • Automatic tensor to NumPy conversion
  • Optional Top-K prediction support
  • Easy integration into inference pipelines

This abstraction simplifies the handling of classification outputs and ensures consistent processing across different models.

docs.title: Authors
docs.text: Atharva Kotkar Aarav Agrawal MIT Internship – Swatah AI

docs.title: Detections Core API
docs.text:
This document explains how the Detections class in SwatahVision works.

The Detections class is the core data structure used for object detection results. It stores information about detected objects such as:

  • Bounding boxes
  • Confidence scores
  • Class IDs
  • Segmentation masks
  • Tracking IDs
  • Additional metadata

It also provides utilities for:

  • Filtering detections
  • Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)
  • Non-Maximum Merging (NMM)
  • Merging detections from multiple models
  • Accessing anchor positions of objects

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install the required libraries.

pip install swatahvision
pip install numpy
pip install opencv-python

docs.title: Import Libraries
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv
import numpy as np

docs.title: What is the Detections Class?
docs.text:
The Detections class stores information about detected objects.

Each detection may contain:

Field Description xyxy Bounding box coordinates [x1, y1, x2, y2] confidence Detection confidence score class_id Predicted object class mask Segmentation mask (optional) tracker_id Object tracking ID (optional) data Additional user data metadata Extra information

docs.title: Creating a Detections Object
docs.text:
Example:

import numpy as np
import swatahvision as sv

detections = sv.Detections(
    xyxy=np.array([[50, 60, 200, 220]]),
    confidence=np.array([0.89]),
    class_id=np.array([1])
)

print(detections)

docs.title: Creating Detections from Model Outputs
docs.text: SwatahVision provides helper methods to convert model outputs.

docs.title: YOLO
docs.text:
detections = sv.Detections.from_yolo(outputs, conf_threshold=0.5)

This converts YOLO predictions into a Detections object.

Parameters:

Parameter Description outputs Raw model output conf_threshold Minimum confidence score nms_threshold Optional NMS threshold class_agnostic Ignore class IDs during NMS

docs.title: SSD
docs.text:
detections = sv.Detections.from_ssd(outputs, conf_threshold=0.5)

This converts SSD predictions into structured detections.

docs.title: RetinaNet
docs.text:
detections = sv.Detections.from_retinanet(outputs, conf_threshold=0.5)

This converts RetinaNet predictions into Detections.

docs.title: Iterating Through Detections
docs.text:
You can loop through all detections.

for xyxy, mask, confidence, class_id, tracker_id, data in detections:
    print("Box:", xyxy)
    print("Confidence:", confidence)
    print("Class:", class_id)

Example output:

Box: [45 60 210 220]
Confidence: 0.91
Class: 2

docs.title: Get Number of Detections
docs.text:
print(len(detections))

Example output:

3

docs.title: Access Specific Detections
docs.text:
Get the first detection:

first = detections[0]

Get first 10 detections:

subset = detections[0:10]

Filter detections:

high_conf = detections[detections.confidence > 0.7]

Filter by class:

persons = detections[detections.class_id == 0]

docs.title: Adding Custom Data
docs.text:
You can attach additional information.

Example:

detections["names"] = ["car", "person", "bicycle"]

This stores extra metadata inside the detection object.

docs.title: Checking Empty Detections
docs.text:
Create empty detections:

detections = sv.Detections.empty()

Check if empty:

detections.is_empty()

docs.title: Merging Detections
docs.text:
You can combine multiple detection sets.

Example:

merged = sv.Detections.merge([detections1, detections2])

This is useful when using multiple detection models.

Example result:

Original model 1 detections : 3
Original model 2 detections : 2

Merged detections : 5

docs.title: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)
docs.text:
Object detection models may detect the same object multiple times.

NMS removes duplicate detections.

filtered = detections.with_nms(threshold=0.5)

Parameters:

Parameter Description threshold IoU threshold class_agnostic Ignore class IDs overlap_metric Overlap metric (IOU or IOS)

docs.title: Non-Maximum Merging (NMM)
docs.text:
NMM merges overlapping detections instead of removing them.

merged = detections.with_nmm(threshold=0.5)

This combines multiple overlapping boxes into one larger detection.

docs.title: Getting Anchor Coordinates
docs.text:
You can get anchor positions inside bounding boxes.

Example:

from swatahvision.geometry.core import Position

centers = detections.get_anchors_coordinates(Position.CENTER)
print(centers)

Supported anchor positions:

Anchor Description CENTER center of box TOP_LEFT top left corner TOP_RIGHT top right corner BOTTOM_LEFT bottom left corner BOTTOM_RIGHT bottom right corner CENTER_LEFT left center CENTER_RIGHT right center BOTTOM_CENTER bottom center

docs.title: Example Full Workflow
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv

model = sv.Model(
    model="yolov8n.xml",
    engine=sv.Engine.OPENVINO,
    hardware=sv.Hardware.CPU
)

image = sv.Image.load_from_file("image.jpg")

outputs = model(image)

detections = sv.Detections.from_yolo(outputs, conf_threshold=0.5)

detections = detections.with_nms(threshold=0.5)

for box, mask, confidence, class_id, tracker_id, data in detections:
    print("Object:", class_id, "Confidence:", confidence)

Example output:

Object: 2 Confidence: 0.91
Object: 0 Confidence: 0.87

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The Detections class is the core structure used by SwatahVision for object detection results.

It helps developers to:

  • Store detection results
  • Convert model outputs
  • Filter detections
  • Remove duplicate detections
  • Merge results from multiple models
  • Access bounding box positions

This makes it easier to build computer vision pipelines for real-world applications.

docs.title: Authors
docs.text: Atharva Kotkar Aarav Agrawal MIT Internship – Swatah AI

docs.title: Age & Gender Prediction using SwatahVision (OpenVINO Engine)
docs.text:
This example demonstrates how to perform Age and Gender prediction using the age-gender-recognition-retail-0013 model through the SwatahVision framework, powered internally by the OpenVINO engine.

The script loads two face images, runs inference on CPU, and prints the predicted age and gender in the terminal.

docs.title: 📁 Folder Structure
docs.text:
age_gender_project/
├── age-gender-recognition-retail-0013.xml
├── age-gender-recognition-retail-0013.bin
├── age_gender_prediction.py
├── README.md
├── image_old_man.jpg
└── image_boy.jpg

docs.title: 📥 Model Download
docs.text:
Download the model from Hugging Face:

🔗 Model Repository: https://huggingface.co/swatah/swatahvision/tree/main/classifiation/age-gender-recognition-retail-0013

Download the following files:

  • age-gender-recognition-retail-0013.xml
  • age-gender-recognition-retail-0013.bin

Place both files inside your project root directory.

docs.title: 🔧 Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+
  • NumPy
  • OpenCV
  • SwatahVision

docs.title: Create Environment (Recommended)
docs.text:
conda create -n swatah_env python=3.9 -y
conda activate swatah_env

docs.title: Install Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install numpy
pip install opencv-python
pip install swatahvision

docs.title: 🚀 How to Run
docs.text: python age_gender_prediction.py

docs.title: 🧪 Complete Source Code
docs.text:
Below is the exact code used in this project:

import swatahvision as sv
import numpy as np
import cv2

MODEL_PATH = "age-gender-recognition-retail-0013.xml"
IMAGE_PATH_OLD = "image_old_man.jpg"
IMAGE_PATH_BOY = "image_boy.jpg"

model = sv.Model(
    model=MODEL_PATH,
    engine=sv.Engine.OPENVINO,
    hardware=sv.Hardware.CPU
)

# -----------------------------
# First Image Prediction
# -----------------------------
img = sv.Image.load_from_file(IMAGE_PATH_OLD)
outputs = model(img)[0]

gender_blob = outputs[0]
age_blob = outputs[1]

gender_id = int(np.argmax(gender_blob))
gender = "Male" if gender_id == 1 else "Female"
age = int(age_blob[0][0][0][0] * 100)

print("Predicted Age   :", age)
print("Predicted Gender:", gender)

# -----------------------------
# Second Image Prediction
# -----------------------------
image = sv.Image.load_from_file(IMAGE_PATH_BOY)
outputs = model(image)[0]
print(outputs)

gender_blob = outputs[0]
age_blob = outputs[1]

gender_id = int(np.argmax(gender_blob))
gender = "Male" if gender_id == 1 else "Female"
age = int(age_blob[0][0][0][0] * 100)

print("Predicted Age   :", age)
print("Predicted Gender:", gender)

docs.title: 📤 Sample Output
docs.text:
Predicted Age   : 62
Predicted Gender: Male

Predicted Age   : 14
Predicted Gender: Male

docs.title: 🧠 Model Information
docs.text:
• Model Name: age-gender-recognition-retail-0013
  • Framework: SwatahVision
  • Inference Engine: OpenVINO (internal)
  • Hardware: CPU
  • Age Output: Normalized value × 100
  • Gender Output: Probability scores [Female, Male]

docs.title: ⚠️ Notes
docs.text:
• The model expects cropped face images.
  • Only single-face inference is supported.
  • Age prediction is an estimate.
  • Accuracy depends on lighting and image quality.

docs.title: 📍 Count People in Zone using YOLO (ONNX) + ByteTrack
docs.text:
This example demonstrates how to count the number of people inside predefined polygon zones using computer vision.

It detects and tracks people in a video and determines how many individuals are present inside each defined zone in real-time.

This project is ideal for:

  • 🏬 Retail store occupancy monitoring
  • 🚪 Entry/exit people counting
  • 🏫 Classroom or hall occupancy tracking
  • 🚧 Restricted area monitoring
  • 🧑‍🤝‍🧑 Crowd density estimation

docs.title: 🚀 What This Project Does
docs.text:
The system:

  1. Detects people using a YOLO ONNX model
  2. Tracks individuals using ByteTrack
  3. Monitors predefined polygon zones
  4. Checks whether a tracked person is inside a zone
  5. Counts the number of people inside each zone
  6. Displays the count in real-time
  7. Saves the annotated output video Each zone displays the number of people currently inside it.

Example:

Zone 1: 5
Zone 2: 2

Means:

5 people are inside Zone 1
2 people are inside Zone 2
The count updates dynamically as people enter or leave the zone.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/VisionAI4Bharat/swatahvision.git
cd swatahvision/examples/count_people_in_zone

docs.title: 2️⃣ (Optional) Create Virtual Environment
docs.text:
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: 3️⃣ Install Dependencies
docs.text: pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 🎨 Step 1: Draw Custom Zones
docs.text:
Before running people counting, you must define monitoring zones.

Use the provided script:

python scripts/draw_zones.py \
    --source_path "data/input.mp4" \
    --zone_configuration_path "data/config.json"

docs.title: Controls While Drawing
docs.text:
Key Action Enter Finish current polygon Escape Cancel current polygon s Save zones to JSON file q Quit drawing window

This generates a JSON file containing polygon coordinates.

Example config.json:

[
    [[100,100], [400,100], [400,400], [100,400]]
]

Each polygon represents one monitoring zone.

docs.title: 🧠 Step 2: Run People Counting
docs.text:
Run the detection and tracking script:

python yolov11x-1280_onnx.py \
    --zone_configuration_path "data/config.json" \
    --source_video_path "data/input.mp4" \
    --source_weights_path "yolov11x-1280.onnx" \
    --classes 0 \
    --confidence_threshold 0.3 \
    --iou_threshold 0.7

docs.title: Required
docs.text:
--zone_configuration_path Path to zone configuration JSON file.

--source_video_path Path to input video file.

docs.title: Optional
docs.text:
--source_weights_path Path to YOLO ONNX model file.

--classes List of class IDs to track. Example:

--classes 0
Class 0 corresponds to person detection in most YOLO models. If empty, all detected classes are tracked.

--confidence_threshold Detection confidence threshold. Default: 0.3

--iou_threshold IoU threshold for Non-Max Suppression. Default: 0.7

docs.title: 🧠 How It Works (Technical Overview)
docs.text:
Processing Pipeline:

  1. Load YOLO ONNX model
  2. Read video frames
  3. Run object detection
  4. Filter selected classes (typically person)
  5. Apply ByteTrack multi-object tracking
  6. Compute the center point of each tracked object
  7. Check if object center lies inside polygon zone
  8. Count number of people inside each zone
  9. Display counts on the video
  10. Save the output video

docs.title: 👥 People Counting Logic
docs.text:
People counting is performed by checking whether the center point of a tracked bounding box lies inside a polygon zone.

If the center point lies inside the zone, the person is counted as present in that zone.

The count updates continuously for each frame.

If a person leaves the zone, the count decreases automatically.

docs.title: 📊 Output
docs.text:
The output video contains:

  • Drawn polygon zones
  • Tracked object IDs
  • Real-time people count per zone
  • Bounding boxes around detected people
  • Saved annotated output video

docs.title: 🖥 Model Requirements
docs.text:
This project requires a YOLO ONNX model.

You can:

  • Export from Ultralytics YOLO
  • Use a pre-exported ONNX model
  • Use your internal trained model

Example export:

pip install ultralytics
yolo export model=yolov8x.pt format=onnx imgsz=1280

docs.title: 🎯 Practical Applications
docs.text:
• Retail store occupancy monitoring
  • Crowd density analysis
  • Smart building management
  • Security restricted-area monitoring
  • Event crowd management
  • Smart city pedestrian analytics

docs.title: 📌 Notes
docs.text:
• GPU recommended for real-time performance
  • Zones are triggered using object center point
  • Supports multiple zones
  • Supports multiple object classes
  • Works best with fixed camera footage

docs.title: 🔥 Heatmap & Multi-Object Tracking using YOLO (ONNX) + ByteTrack
docs.text:
This project demonstrates crowd movement analysis using a YOLO ONNX detection model and ByteTrack multi-object tracking.

It processes a video to:

  • Detect people
  • Track each person with a unique ID
  • Generate a dynamic heatmap of movement
  • Save an annotated output video

docs.text:
• YOLO ONNX inference (GPU supported)
  • Real-time multi-object tracking (ByteTrack)
  • Person-only filtering (class_id = 0)
  • Accumulated movement heatmap
  • Configurable detection & tracking thresholds

│

weight.onnx

heatmap_and_track/

docs.text:
Basic run:

python yolov11x-1280_onnx.py \
    --source_weights_path weight.onnx

Run with custom video:

python yolov11x-1280_onnx.py \

docs.title: Required

--iou_threshold IoU threshold for NMS. Default: 0.5

--match_threshold Threshold for matching tracks with detections. Default: 0.99

docs.title: 🧠 How It Works

Pipeline:

docs.title: Pose Estimation using MoveNet (ONNX)

Movenet.onnx
  1. Place it inside:

import swatahvision as sv
from pose import Pose

model = sv.Model(

)

outs = model(image)

docs.title: Speed Estimation using YOLO (ONNX) + ByteTrack
docs.text:
This example demonstrates how to estimate the speed of moving objects in a video using computer vision.

The system detects objects in each frame, tracks them across frames, and calculates their speed based on the distance they travel over time.

Speed estimation is widely used in intelligent video analytics systems to analyze motion patterns and monitor moving objects.

This project is ideal for:

  • 🚗 Traffic speed monitorin
  • 🏙 Smart city surveillance systems
  • 🚦 Traffic behavior analysis
  • 🏭 Industrial vehicle monitoring
  • 📊 Motion analytics in security footage

docs.title: 🚀 What This Project Does
docs.text:
The system performs the following steps:

  1. Detects objects using a YOLO ONNX model

  8. Estimate speed based on displacement and time
  9. Display speed annotations on the video

docs.title: 📏 Speed Calculation
docs.text:
Speed is estimated using the relationship between distance and time. Basic formula: 

speed = distance / time
In video analytics, distance is calculated from the pixel displacement between frames. 
speed = pixel_distance / frame_time
Where: - pixel_distance → displacement of the tracked object - frame_time → time between frames (1 / FPS)

The estimated speed can be converted to real-world units such as km/h depending on calibration.

docs.title: Image Classification using MobileNetV2 (OpenVINO Engine)
docs.text:
This example demonstrates how to perform image classification using the MobileNetV2 model through the SwatahVision framework, powered internally by the OpenVINO engine.

The script loads an input image, runs inference on CPU, and prints the top-5 predicted classes in the terminal.

  • Example: assets/car.jpg

You may use any object image for classification.

docs.title: 🔧 Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+
  • NumPy
  • OpenCV
  • SwatahVision

OpenVINO is used internally by SwatahVision. You do NOT need to write OpenVINO code manually.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Create Environment (Recommended)
docs.text:
conda create -n swatah_env python=3.9 -y
conda activate swatah_env

docs.title: 2️⃣ Install Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install numpy
pip install opencv-python
pip install swatahvision

docs.title: 🚀 How to Run
docs.text: python mobilenet_classification.py

docs.title: 🧪 Complete Source Code
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv

model = sv.Model(
    model="mobilenetv2.xml",
    engine=sv.Engine.OPENVINO,
    hardware=sv.Hardware.CPU
)

image = sv.Image.load_from_file("assets/car.jpg")

outs = model(image)

classification = sv.Classification.from_mobilenet(outs, top_k=5)

print(classification)

# Configure bounding box annotation
# ---------------------------------------------
box_annotator = sv.BoxAnnotator(sv.Color.YELLOW)

# ---------------------------------------------
# Load SSD Lite MobileNetV3 model
# - OpenVino runtime
# - CPU inference
# ---------------------------------------------
model = sv.Model(
    model="retinanet-resnet50-fpn.xml", engine=sv.Engine.OPENVINO, hardware=sv.Hardware.CPU
)

# ---------------------------------------------
# Load input image from file
# ---------------------------------------------
image = sv.Image.load_from_file("assets/car.jpg")

# ---------------------------------------------
# Run object detection on the image
# ---------------------------------------------
outs = model(image)

# ---------------------------------------------
# Convert model output to detections
# Apply confidence threshold
# ---------------------------------------------
detections = sv.Detections.from_retinanet(outs, conf_threshold=0.5)

# ---------------------------------------------
# Draw labels (class name, confidence, etc.)
# ---------------------------------------------
image = label_annotator.annotate(scene=image, detections=detections)

# ---------------------------------------------
# Draw bounding boxes on the image
# ---------------------------------------------
image = box_annotator.annotate(scene=image, detections=detections)

# ---------------------------------------------
# Display the final annotated image
# ---------------------------------------------
sv.Image.show(image=image)

docs.title: ONNX Runtime Engine for SwatahVision
docs.text:
This code creates a custom inference engine that allows models in ONNX format to run inside the SwatahVision framework.

In simple terms, it helps the system:

  1. Load an ONNX model
  2. Prepare an image so the model can understand it
  3. Run the model on the image
  4. Return the prediction results

docs.title: What is ONNX?
docs.text:
ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) is a format used to store machine learning models.

It allows models created in frameworks like:

  • PyTorch
  • TensorFlow
  • Keras

to run in many different environments.

This code uses ONNX Runtime, which is a tool that executes ONNX models efficiently.

docs.title: Main Purpose of This Code
docs.text:
This file creates a class called:

OnnxRuntimeEngine

This class connects:

SwatahVision → ONNX Runtime

So that SwatahVision can run ONNX models easily.

docs.title: Libraries Used
docs.text:
The code uses the following Python libraries:

  • onnxruntime → runs ONNX models
  • numpy → handles numerical data
  • opencv (cv2) → processes images

docs.title: Class: OnnxRuntimeEngine
docs.text:
This class inherits from the base engine:

RuntimeEngine

It defines how:

  • the model is loaded
  • the input image is prepared
  • the model prediction is executed

docs.title: 1. Loading the Model
docs.text:
Function:

load()

This function loads the ONNX model into memory.

It also selects the hardware used to run the model.

Possible hardware options:

  • CPU
  • GPU

Example behavior:

CPU → CPUExecutionProvider
GPU → CUDAExecutionProvider

After loading the model, the function also collects information about:

  • model inputs
  • input shapes
  • input data types
  • model outputs

docs.title: 2. Running the Model (Inference)
docs.text:
Function:

infer()

This function performs the main prediction.

Steps performed:

  1. Take the input image
  2. Prepare the image using preprocessing
  3. Send the image to the model
  4. Get the prediction result

The model then returns:

  • raw output
  • metadata information

docs.title: 3. Getting Model Information
docs.text:
Function:

get_model_info()

This function reads details from the ONNX model.

It extracts information such as:

  • Input names
  • Input shapes
  • Input data types
  • Output names

This helps the engine understand what format the model expects.

docs.title: 4. Image Preprocessing
docs.text:
Function:

preprocess()

Before sending an image to the model, it must be prepared correctly.

This function performs that preparation.

The steps include:

docs.title: 1. Resize the image
docs.text: The image is resized to match the model's expected input size.

docs.title: 2. Maintain aspect ratio (Letterbox)
docs.text:
Instead of stretching the image, the code keeps the original shape.

It does this by:

  • resizing the image
  • adding padding around it

This process is called letterboxing.

docs.title: 3. Change image format
docs.text:
Images normally look like this:

(H, W, C)
Height, Width, Channels

But models expect:

(C, H, W)
Channels, Height, Width

So the code rearranges the image format.

docs.title: 4. Handle batch inputs
docs.text:
The code can process:

  • single image
  • multiple images at once

If needed, it automatically adds a batch dimension.

docs.title: 5. Convert data type
docs.text:
The model may expect input as:

  • float32
  • uint8

The code converts the image to the correct type.

docs.title: Letterbox Function (Important)
docs.text:
The letterbox() function resizes an image without distortion.

Steps:

  1. Calculate scaling factor
  2. Resize image
  3. Add padding to reach required size

Example:

Original Image

400 × 300

Model Input

640 × 640

The code:

  • resizes the image
  • adds black padding around it

This keeps the image proportions correct.

docs.title: Output of Preprocessing
docs.text:
The preprocessing function returns two things:

processed_image
meta_information

The metadata contains:

  • scale factor
  • padding values

This information can later be used to adjust predictions back to the original image size.

docs.title: Overall Workflow
docs.text:
The complete process looks like this:

Load Model
      ↓
Receive Image
      ↓
Preprocess Image
      ↓
Run Model
      ↓
Return Prediction

docs.title: Why This Code Is Useful
docs.text:
This engine allows:

  • Running ONNX models inside SwatahVision
  • Supporting both CPU and GPU
  • Automatically handling image preprocessing
  • Supporting single and batch inputs

It simplifies the process of deploying ONNX models in computer vision applications.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This code builds a bridge between:

SwatahVision Framework
        ↓
ONNX Runtime Engine
        ↓
Machine Learning Model

It makes it easier to:

  • load models
  • prepare images
  • run predictions
  • retrieve results

docs.title: OpenVINO Runtime Engine for SwatahVision
docs.text:
This code creates a custom runtime engine that allows OpenVINO models to run inside the SwatahVision framework.

In simple terms, this code helps the system:

  1. Load an OpenVINO model
  2. Prepare an image so the model can understand it
  3. Run the model on the image
  4. Return the prediction results

docs.title: What is OpenVINO?
docs.text:
OpenVINO is a toolkit developed by Intel that helps run AI models faster and more efficiently, especially on:

  • CPUs
  • GPUs
  • Intel hardware

It is commonly used for computer vision tasks such as:

  • Image classification
  • Object detection
  • Face recognition

docs.title: Purpose of This Code
docs.text:
This file creates a class called:

OpenVinoRuntimeEngine

This class connects:

SwatahVision → OpenVINO Runtime

This allows SwatahVision to run OpenVINO models easily.

docs.title: Libraries Used
docs.text:
This code uses the following Python libraries:

  • openvino → runs the OpenVINO model
  • numpy → handles numerical data
  • opencv (cv2) → processes images

docs.title: Class: OpenVinoRuntimeEngine
docs.text:
This class extends the base class:

RuntimeEngine

It defines how the system should:

  • load a model
  • prepare the input image
  • run the model
  • return the results

docs.title: 1. Loading the Model
docs.text:
Function:

load()

This function loads the OpenVINO model into memory.

Steps performed:

  1. Select the hardware (CPU or GPU)
  2. Create an OpenVINO Core object
  3. Read the model file
  4. Compile the model for the selected device

Example hardware options:

CPU → runs on processor
GPU → runs on graphics card

After loading the model, the function also collects information about:

  • input names
  • input shapes
  • input data types
  • output names

docs.title: 2. Running the Model (Inference)
docs.text:
Function:

infer()

This function performs the actual prediction.

Steps:

  1. Receive an input image
  2. Prepare the image using preprocessing
  3. Send the image to the compiled model
  4. Get the prediction results

The function returns:

raw_output
meta_information

docs.title: 3. Getting Model Information
docs.text:
Function:

get_model_info()

This function extracts important details from the model.

It collects:

  • Input names
  • Input shapes
  • Input data types
  • Output names

This information helps the engine understand what format the model expects.

docs.title: 4. Image Preprocessing
docs.text:
Function:

preprocess()

Before sending an image to the model, the image must be prepared correctly.

This function performs several steps to prepare the image.

docs.title: Step 1: Resize the Image
docs.text: The image is resized to match the size expected by the model.

docs.title: Step 2: Keep Image Proportions (Letterbox)
docs.text:
Instead of stretching the image, the code keeps the original shape.

This is done using a technique called letterboxing.

The process:

  1. Resize the image
  2. Add padding around the image

This prevents distortion.

docs.title: Step 3: Rearrange Image Format
docs.text:
Normally images are stored as:

(H, W, C)
Height, Width, Channels

But most AI models expect:

(C, H, W)
Channels, Height, Width

So the code rearranges the image format.

docs.title: Step 4: Handle Single or Multiple Images
docs.text:
The engine can process:

  • a single image
  • multiple images at once

If needed, the code automatically adds a batch dimension.

docs.title: Step 5: Convert Image Data Type
docs.text:
Different models expect different input types such as:

  • float32
  • uint8

The code automatically converts the image to the correct type.

docs.title: Letterbox Function
docs.text:
The letterbox() function resizes an image without stretching it.

Steps performed:

  1. Calculate how much the image should scale
  2. Resize the image
  3. Add padding to match the required size

Example:

Original image:

400 × 300

Model input size:

640 × 640

The code:

  • resizes the image
  • adds padding around it

This keeps the image looking natural.

docs.title: Metadata Returned
docs.text:
The preprocessing function also returns metadata information.

This includes:

scale
padding_x
padding_y

This data can later be used to adjust predictions back to the original image size.

docs.title: Overall Workflow
docs.text:
The full process works like this:

Load Model
      ↓
Receive Image
      ↓
Preprocess Image
      ↓
Run Model
      ↓
Return Prediction

docs.title: Why This Code is Useful
docs.text:
This engine makes it easier to run OpenVINO models in SwatahVision.

It provides:

  • automatic image preprocessing
  • support for CPU and GPU
  • support for batch inputs
  • simple model execution

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This code acts as a bridge between:

SwatahVision Framework
        ↓
OpenVINO Runtime Engine
        ↓
AI Model

It simplifies the process of:

  • loading models
  • preparing images
  • running predictions
  • retrieving results

This makes it easier to build computer vision applications using OpenVINO models.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
ByteTrack is a high-performance multi-object tracking algorithm used to track objects across video frames.

It works by associating detections from object detection models with existing tracks and assigning a unique tracker ID to each object.

ByteTrack is widely used in:

  • Object tracking
  • Surveillance systems
  • Traffic monitoring
  • Sports analytics
  • Crowd analysis

The tracker works seamlessly with SwatahVision Detections.

docs.title: Class: ByteTrack
docs.text:
class ByteTrack

The ByteTrack class performs multi-object tracking using bounding box detections.

It assigns persistent IDs to objects across frames.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
ByteTrack(
    track_activation_threshold=0.25,
    lost_track_buffer=30,
    minimum_matching_threshold=0.8,
    frame_rate=30,
    minimum_consecutive_frames=1
)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description track_activation_threshold float Minimum detection confidence required to activate a track lost_track_buffer int Number of frames a track is kept after it disappears minimum_matching_threshold float IOU threshold used when matching tracks with detections frame_rate int Frame rate of the processed video minimum_consecutive_frames int Number of frames an object must appear before being considered a valid track

docs.title: How ByteTrack Works
docs.text:
ByteTrack tracks objects using the following pipeline:

  1. Object Detection

Objects are detected using models such as:

  • YOLO
  • SSD

  • RetinaNet

  • Bounding Box Association

Detected boxes are matched with existing tracks using IoU distance.

  1. Kalman Filter Prediction

Object motion is predicted using a Kalman Filter.

  1. Track Management

Tracks are categorized as:

  • Tracked – currently active objects
  • Lost – temporarily missing objects
  • Removed – expired tracks

docs.title: Method: update_with_detections
docs.text:
update_with_detections(detections: Detections) -> Detections

Updates the tracker using detection results and returns updated detections with tracker IDs.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description detections Detections Detection results containing bounding boxes and confidence scores

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Detections

Updated detections with tracker_id assigned.

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
import swatahvision as sv
from ultralytics import YOLO

model = YOLO("yolov8n.pt")
tracker = sv.ByteTrack()

box_annotator = sv.BoxAnnotator()
label_annotator = sv.LabelAnnotator()

def callback(frame, index):

    results = model(frame)[0]
    detections = sv.Detections.from_ultralytics(results)

    detections = tracker.update_with_detections(detections)

    labels = [f"#{tracker_id}" for tracker_id in detections.tracker_id]

    frame = box_annotator.annotate(
        scene=frame.copy(),
        detections=detections
    )

    frame = label_annotator.annotate(
        scene=frame,
        detections=detections,
        labels=labels
    )

    return frame

docs.title: Reset Tracker
docs.text:
tracker.reset()

Resets the internal state of the tracker.

This clears:

  • tracked objects
  • lost objects
  • removed objects

Useful when processing multiple videos sequentially.

docs.title: Internal Tracking Pipeline
docs.text: ByteTrack internally performs several steps:

docs.title: 1. Detection Filtering
docs.text: Detections are separated based on confidence thresholds.

docs.title: 2. First Association
docs.text: High confidence detections are matched with existing tracks.

docs.title: 3. Second Association
docs.text: Lower confidence detections are matched to recover lost tracks.

docs.title: 4. Track Initialization
docs.text: New tracks are created for unmatched detections.

docs.title: 5. Track Removal
docs.text: Tracks that remain lost for too long are removed.

docs.title: Track States
docs.text:
Tracks can exist in three states:

State Description Tracked Object is currently visible Lost Object temporarily disappeared Removed Object removed after long absence

docs.title: Helper Functions
docs.text: ByteTrack uses several internal helper functions.

docs.title: joint_tracks
docs.text:
joint_tracks(track_list_a, track_list_b)

Combines two track lists while avoiding duplicate tracks.

docs.title: sub_tracks
docs.text:
sub_tracks(track_list_a, track_list_b)

Removes tracks from one list that exist in another list.

docs.title: remove_duplicate_tracks
docs.text:
remove_duplicate_tracks(tracks_a, tracks_b)

Removes duplicate tracks based on IoU similarity.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
ByteTrack relies on the following modules:

  • NumPy
  • Kalman Filter
  • IoU matching utilities
  • SwatahVision Detection module

docs.title: Example Visualization
docs.text:
When combined with annotators like:

  • BoxAnnotator
  • LabelAnnotator
  • TraceAnnotator

you can visualize object tracking across frames.

Example output:

Person #1
Person #2
Car #3

Each object keeps the same ID across frames.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The ByteTrack tracker enables reliable multi-object tracking in video streams.

Key features:

  • High accuracy tracking
  • Kalman filter motion prediction
  • Robust handling of occlusions
  • Persistent object IDs
  • Easy integration with detection models

It is an essential component for building real-time computer vision tracking systems.
